title: grpc.aio.Metadata: __delitem__ leaves ghost keys that break __contains__ and the Python container protocol
author: RudraDudhat2509
body:
### Description

`grpc.aio.Metadata.__delitem__` does not remove a key from the internal `OrderedDict` when the last value for that key is deleted,  it sets the key to an empty list instead. This causes `__contains__` (`in` operator) to return `True` for a key that has no retrievable values,  diverging from both `delete_all` and every other language SDK.

### Reproduction

```python
from grpc.aio import Metadata

md = Metadata(("foo", "bar"))
del md["foo"]

print("foo" in md)     # True  ← BUG: ghost key
print(md.get("foo"))   # None
print(list(md))        # []
print(len(md))         # 0

# Breaks the standard Python container contract:
# "if x in container: container[x]" must not raise KeyError
if "foo" in md:
    md["foo"]          # KeyError!
```

Multi-value case:

```python
md = Metadata(("foo", "bar"), ("foo", "baz"))
del md["foo"]  # removes "bar"
del md["foo"]  # removes "baz", empty list remains

print("foo" in md)    # True  ← ghost key
print(md.get("foo"))  # None
print(list(md))       # []
```

### Root cause

`__delitem__` in `src/python/grpcio/grpc/aio/_metadata.py`:

```python
def __delitem__(self, key: MetadataKey) -> None:
    current_values = self.get_all(key)
    if not current_values:
        raise KeyError(repr(key))
    self._metadata[key] = current_values[1:]  # ← leaves empty list when last value removed
```

`__contains__` checks `key in self._metadata` (whether the key exists in the dict at all), not whether there are values. After the last value is deleted, the key remains as `[]`, so `__contains__` returns `True`.

### Inconsistency with `delete_all`

The two deletion methods leave the object in inconsistent states:

```python
md1 = Metadata(("foo", "bar"))
del md1["foo"]
print("foo" in md1)   # True  ← ghost key

md2 = Metadata(("foo", "bar"))
md2.delete_all("foo")
print("foo" in md2)   # False ← correct
```

`delete_all` uses `del self._metadata[key]` which removes the key entirely. `__delitem__` does not.

### How other language SDKs handle this

**Go (`grpc-go`)**  uses real map deletion, no ghost keys possible:
```go
func (md MD) Delete(k string) {
    k = strings.ToLower(k)
    delete(md, k)  // key fully removed
}
```

**Java (`grpc-java`)**  compacts the internal array, key is physically removed:
```java
public <T> Iterable<T> removeAll(Key<T> key) {
    // entries for that key are removed from the namesAndValues array entirely
    // containsKey scans the array — if absent, returns false
}
```

Python `grpc.aio.Metadata` is the only implementation that leaves a ghost key after deletion.

### Proposed fix

In `__delitem__`, remove the key entirely when the remaining list is empty:

```python
def __delitem__(self, key: MetadataKey) -> None:
    current_values = self.get_all(key)
    if not current_values:
        raise KeyError(repr(key))
    remaining = current_values[1:]
    if remaining:
        self._metadata[key] = remaining
    else:
        del self._metadata[key]
```

### Environment

- grpcio version: 1.80.0
- Python: 3.x
- File: `src/python/grpcio/grpc/aio/_metadata.py`

title: [CI] Upgrade debian-based docker image to 12/13
author: yuanweiz
body:
Context: foundational C++ policy will be [updated](https://github.com/google/oss-policies-info/pull/68) and we only need to support debian 13 (currently we're using debian11/12 images). To make things easier, I think we can upgrade most tests from debian 11 -> 12 first and then evaluate the difficulty of upgrading from 12 -> 13.

Rationale: debian 12 already comes with compilers/toolings that are:
* new enough for us to build against latest dependencies:
  * boringssl@0.20260616.0  (requires cmake 3.22)
  * absl@20260526.0 (expliclity requires gcc 10, and implicitly clang > 10 due to compiler bug)
  * protobuf@35.1
* old enough to cover some lower bounds of fundational requirement
  * clang 14
* Works well with existing tests
  * some debian11/12 based python tests are relying on downgrading system pip to 19.3.1. This may not work on debian 13.

The default package versions on debian 12. This should should suffice the need of most of our tests.

```
root@ff9f2e2803e1:/var/tmp# dpkg-query --showformat='${Package}: ${Version}\n' --show cmake ccache clang gcc python3
ccache: 4.8+really4.7.5-1
clang: 1:14.0-55.7~deb12u1
cmake: 3.25.1-1
gcc: 4:12.2.0-3
python3: 3.11.2-1+b1
```

title: [CI][2026Q3] Comply with OSS Foundational C++ Support policies
author: yuanweiz
body:
## Requirements

* Debian (next change 2026-06-30)
* macOS (next change 2026-07-01)
* Cmake >= 3.22
* GCC (next change 2026-06-30)
* Apple Clang (next change 2026-07-01)
* MSVC 2022 (drop all references to 2019)

title: [Python] xds-protos bundles a stale copy of opentelemetry-proto, breaking the opentelemetry-proto package
author: avelychko12
body:
### What version of gRPC and what language are you using?

`xds-protos 1.76.0` and `opentelemetry-proto 1.39.0`, Python.

### What operating system (Linux, Windows,...) and version?

Not OS-specific — reproducible on any platform (originally seen on Linux, Python 3.13).

### What runtime / compiler are you using (e.g. python version or version of gcc)

Python 3.13

### What did you do?

The `xds-protos` package ships generated modules under the `opentelemetry.proto.*` namespace — see [`opentelemetry/proto/trace/v1/trace_pb2.py`](https://github.com/grpc/grpc/blob/46b1795eacc99065c46e3fb60fad808c19f71a87/tools/distrib/python/xds_protos/opentelemetry/proto/trace/v1/trace_pb2.py#L29). These same modules are the entire contents of the standalone [`opentelemetry-proto`](https://pypi.org/project/opentelemetry-proto/) package, a common dependency across the OpenTelemetry Python ecosystem.

The copy bundled in `xds-protos` is based on a **much older** revision of [open-telemetry/opentelemetry-proto](https://github.com/open-telemetry/opentelemetry-proto). For example, its `common.proto` still defines `InstrumentationLibrary`, whereas current `opentelemetry-proto` defines `InstrumentationScope`.

Because both distributions claim the same import path (`opentelemetry/proto/...`), whichever is installed **last** overwrites the other's files. Installing `xds-protos` after `opentelemetry-proto` replaces the current generated code with the stale copy:

```bash
$ docker run --rm -it ghcr.io/astral-sh/uv:python3.13-alpine /bin/sh

# opentelemetry-proto alone — fine
/ # uvx --python 3.13 --with opentelemetry-proto \
    python -c "from opentelemetry.proto.common.v1.common_pb2 import InstrumentationScope; print('ok')"
ok

# add xds-protos — the import now breaks
/ # uvx --python 3.13 --with opentelemetry-proto --with xds-protos \
    python -c "from opentelemetry.proto.common.v1.common_pb2 import InstrumentationScope; print('ok')"
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
ImportError: cannot import name 'InstrumentationScope' from 'opentelemetry.proto.common.v1.common_pb2'

# the stale copy provides the old symbol instead
/ # uvx --python 3.13 --with opentelemetry-proto --with xds-protos \
    python -c "from opentelemetry.proto.common.v1.common_pb2 import InstrumentationLibrary; print('ok')"
ok
```

Reinstalling `opentelemetry-proto` afterwards restores the correct files and fixes the import — confirming this is last-writer-wins on the shared files, not a logic bug in either package.

### What did you expect to see?

Installing or uninstalling `xds-protos` should not affect modules provided by `opentelemetry-proto`. Imports from `opentelemetry.proto.*` should resolve to the same code regardless of install order.

### What did you see instead?

Three failure modes (identical in nature to #42830, which covers the `google.*` overlap):

1. **Last writer wins** — the same `opentelemetry.proto.*` import resolves to different generated code depending on install order, and can break entirely (missing symbols, e.g. `InstrumentationScope`).
2. **Uninstall breaks the survivor** — removing `xds-protos` deletes the shared files and breaks `opentelemetry-proto` even though it's still installed.
3. **Silent version skew / descriptor conflicts** — the bundled protos diverge from the real `opentelemetry-proto` version.

### Anything else we should know about your project / environment?

This is the same root cause as #42830 (`xds-protos` bundling externally-owned namespaces), but for `opentelemetry.*` rather than `google.*`. Note that #42830's proposed fix lists `opentelemetry.*` among the namespaces "unique to xds-protos" that should keep being bundled — it isn't unique; it's owned by the standalone `opentelemetry-proto` package, so it needs the same treatment as `google.*`.

Suggested fix: add `opentelemetry-proto` as a dependency of `xds-protos` and stop bundling the overlapping `opentelemetry.proto.*` modules, shipping only the namespaces genuinely unique to xds-protos (`envoy.*`, `xds.*`, `udpa.*`, `cel.*`, `validate.*`, etc.).

Originally reported at open-telemetry/opentelemetry-python#4843, where the maintainers confirmed the collision originates in `xds-protos`.

title: grpc==1.82.0 errors at runtime with protobuf < 7 but pip lets you install this combination
author: harry-dnv
body:
<!--
PLEASE DO NOT POST A QUESTION HERE.
This form is for bug reports and feature requests ONLY!

For general questions and troubleshooting, please ask/look for answers at StackOverflow, with "grpc" tag: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/tagged/grpc

For questions that specifically need to be answered by gRPC team members, please ask/look for answers at grpc.io mailing list: https://groups.google.com/forum/#!forum/grpc-io

Issues specific to *grpc-java*, *grpc-go*, *grpc-node*, *grpc-dart*, *grpc-web* should be created in the repository they belong to (e.g. https://github.com/grpc/grpc-LANGUAGE/issues/new)
-->

### What version of gRPC and what language are you using?
gRPC=1.82.0

### What operating system (Linux, Windows,...) and version?
Linux Ubuntu LTS

### What runtime / compiler are you using (e.g. python version or version of gcc)
python=3.11

### What did you do?
```
$ uv venv \
&& source .venv/bin/activate \
&& uv pip install "protobuf<7" "grpcio-health-checking" \
&& python -c 'import google.protobuf; print("protobuf runtime:", google.protobuf.__version__); from grpc_health.v1 import health_pb2'
```



### What did you expect to see?
No error on import, OR for grpcio to report on `pip install` that it was not compatible with `protobuf<7`

### What did you see instead?
```
Using CPython 3.11.15 interpreter at: /usr/local/bin/python
Creating virtual environment at: .venv
Resolved 4 packages in 10ms
Installed 4 packages in 62ms
 + grpcio==1.82.0
 + grpcio-health-checking==1.82.0
 + protobuf==6.33.6
 + typing-extensions==4.16.0
protobuf runtime: 6.33.6
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/app/tmp/.venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/grpc_health/v1/health_pb2.py", line 12, in <module>
    _runtime_version.ValidateProtobufRuntimeVersion(
  File "/app/tmp/.venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/google/protobuf/runtime_version.py", line 100, in ValidateProtobufRuntimeVersion
    _ReportVersionError(
  File "/app/tmp/.venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/google/protobuf/runtime_version.py", line 50, in _ReportVersionError
    raise VersionError(msg)
google.protobuf.runtime_version.VersionError: Detected incompatible Protobuf Gencode/Runtime versions when loading grpc_health/v1/health.proto: gencode 7.35.0 runtime 6.33.6. Runtime version cannot be older than the linked gencode version. See Protobuf version guarantees at https://protobuf.dev/support/cross-version-runtime-guarantee.
```


### Anything else we should know about your project / environment?
```
(tmp) $ uv pip list
Package                Version
---------------------- -------
grpcio                 1.82.0
grpcio-health-checking 1.82.0
protobuf               6.33.6
typing-extensions      4.16.0
```

title: [Core] Update the BoringSSLto a version from at least August 2025 when MLKEM-1024 was added
author: yuanweiz
body:
We're still using boringSSL `0.20250415.0` which is old. The documentation in `third_party/README.md` is also outdated and doesn't reflect the new upstream changes. I've did a bit of research and seems like we'll need to do a few things:

## Build System Requirements
* Upgrade cmake to >= 3.22
* Prefix header isn't needed anymore, `tools/distrib/generate_boringssl_prefix_header.sh` should be deleted.
* Schema of `gen/sources.json` is changed so build system needs to be updated accordingly.

## Platform/Language Specific Issues
* Fix android large-file-support error: `error: no member named 'fseeko' in the global namespace; did you mean 'fseek'`
  * `fseeko` and `ftello` isn't available in some platform. See bionic [doc](https://android.googlesource.com/platform/bionic/+/master/docs/32-bit-abi.md)
  * Example failure https://btx.cloud.google.com/invocations/76b31bf2-4c7e-4e1b-9f63-07df5a61bee6/targets/grpc%2Fcore%2Fpull_request%2Flinux%2Fgrpc_android/log
* Ruby Distrubution Test.
  * `error: member reference base type 'Span' is not a structure or union`, Example [failure](https://btx.cloud.google.com/invocations/e681c163-2a8f-499c-ae8f-1a088e680ec3/targets)
  * Root cause unclear, might have to do with gcc cross compiler version too old (9.4)

title: gRPC 1.81+ shared libraries incompatible with ASAN: ODR violation when instrumented, false container-overflow when not
author: TedLyngmo
body:
### What version of gRPC and what language are you using?
gRPC 1.81.1 (built with `-DBUILD_SHARED_LIBS=ON`, CMake, all providers as module), C++

### What operating system (Linux, Windows,...) and version?
Debian GNU/Linux 13 (trixie)

### What runtime / compiler are you using (e.g. python version or version of gcc)
GCC 14.2, Python 3.13.5

### What did you do (1)?
Built the gRPC 1.81.1 stack from source with cmake **without** ASan and **without** UBSan.
Compiled our own code using ASan and UBSan and linked with the gRPC libraries.

### What did you expect to see (1)?
No ASan and UBSan errors in the gRPC related code.

### What did you see instead (1)?
False container-overflows. If consumer code includes protobuf/abseil headers and IS compiled with ASAN, false container-overflow errors occur when iterating `google::protobuf::RepeatedField`.

Possible root cause: `absl/base/config.h` defines `ABSL_HAVE_ADDRESS_SANITIZER` based on `__SANITIZE_ADDRESS__`, which is set by the compiler when `-fsanitize=address` is active. This causes `ABSL_ANNOTATE_CONTIGUOUS_CONTAINER` (in `absl/base/dynamic_annotations.h`) to expand to `__sanitizer_annotate_contiguous_container()` in inline functions within the protobuf headers (e.g., `RepeatedField::AnnotateSize()`).

The result:
- Consumer code compiles inline `RepeatedField` methods WITH annotations active
- The library populates `RepeatedField` data WITHOUT annotations (because library was built without `-fsanitize=address`)
- ASAN sees annotated boundaries from the inline header code but missing annotations from the library, and reports container-overflow

Example:
```
==7988==ERROR: AddressSanitizer: container-overflow on address 0x50c000118a10
READ of size 4 at 0x50c000118a10
    #0 in getGrpcOptionSet (iterating const RepeatedField<int>& via range-for)
SUMMARY: AddressSanitizer: container-overflow
```
And memcpy interceptor hits:
```
SUMMARY: AddressSanitizer: container-overflow .../sanitizer_common_interceptors_memintrinsics.inc:115 in memcpy
```
### What did you do (2)?
Built the gRPC 1.81.1 stack from source with cmake **with** ASan and UBSan.
Compiled our own code using ASan and UBSan and linked with the gRPC libraries.

### What did you expect to see (2)?
No ODR violations in gRPC

### What did you see instead (2)?
```
==26749==ERROR: AddressSanitizer: odr-violation (0x7fd6e69371c0):
  [1] size=40 'google_protobuf_descriptor_proto_upb_file_layout' .../descriptor.upb_minitable.c:1439 in libgrpc.so.53
  [2] size=40 'google_protobuf_descriptor_proto_upb_file_layout' .../descriptor.upb_minitable.c:1439 in libupb_reflection_lib.so.53
```

### Impact
There is no working configuration for ASAN + gRPC 1.81 shared libraries:
- Build gRPC with ASAN: ODR violation aborts
- Build gRPC without ASAN: container-overflow false positives abort

### Wanted solution
Being able to compile gRPC with ASAN _or_ without ASAN and be able to use it without suppressions like `ASAN_OPTIONS=detect_container_overflow=0`.

### Related
- https://github.com/grpc/grpc/issues/41808 (ODR violation in shared lib builds)
We've already given up on compiling gRPC with UBSan because of https://github.com/abseil/abseil-cpp/issues/1634 but that doesn't seem to cause issues when our consumer code uses UBSan.

title: [Python] `xds-protos` ships `google.*` modules that conflict with `googleapis-common-protos`
author: mahlberg
body:
### What version of gRPC and what language are you using?

`xds-protos 1.81.1` and `googleapis-common-protos 1.75.0`, Python.

### What operating system (Linux, Windows,...) and version?

Not OS-specific — reproducible on any platform.

### What runtime / compiler are you using (e.g. python version or version of gcc)

Python 3.13

### What did you do?

The `xds-protos` package ships 97 generated modules under `google.api`, `google.rpc`, `google.type`, `google.longrunning`, and `google.logging`. These same modules are provided by `googleapis-common-protos`, which is a common transitive dependency (e.g. via `google-cloud-bigquery`, `grpcio-status`, etc.).

When both packages are installed, whichever is installed **last** overwrites the other's files. The generated code differs between the two packages (different protoc versions / proto source revisions), so users get different runtime behavior depending on install order.

To reproduce:

```bash
# Install in order A
pip install googleapis-common-protos xds-protos
python -c "import hashlib; f=open(__import__('google.rpc.status_pb2',fromlist=['.']).status_pb2.__file__,'rb'); print(hashlib.md5(f.read()).hexdigest()[:12])"
# prints: 090c9b83e5b8 (from xds-protos)

# Install in order B
pip install xds-protos googleapis-common-protos
python -c "import hashlib; f=open(__import__('google.rpc.status_pb2',fromlist=['.']).status_pb2.__file__,'rb'); print(hashlib.md5(f.read()).hexdigest()[:12])"
# prints: 4f8f5c7ebd8a (from googleapis-common-protos)
```

Additionally, uninstalling either package removes the shared files and breaks the other — even though it's still installed:

```bash
pip install googleapis-common-protos xds-protos
pip uninstall xds-protos
python -c "import google.rpc.status_pb2"
# ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'google.rpc.status_pb2'
# ...despite googleapis-common-protos still being installed
```

### What did you expect to see?

Deterministic behavior regardless of install order. Installing or uninstalling `xds-protos` should not affect modules provided by `googleapis-common-protos`.

### What did you see instead?

Three failure modes:
1. **Last writer wins** — the same import resolves to different generated code depending on install order.
2. **Uninstall breaks the survivor** — removing one package deletes shared files, breaking the other.
3. **Silent version skew** — if the bundled protos diverge from the `googleapis-common-protos` versions, users can hit protobuf descriptor conflicts at runtime.

### Anything else we should know about your project / environment?

I've verified that all 97 `google.*` modules shipped by `xds-protos` are also available in `googleapis-common-protos`. A possible fix would be to add `googleapis-common-protos` as a dependency of `xds-protos` and stop bundling the overlapping `google.*` modules — only shipping the namespaces unique to xds-protos (`envoy.*`, `xds.*`, `udpa.*`, `opencensus.*`, `opentelemetry.*`, `cel.*`, `validate.*`).

I'm happy to submit a PR for this if the approach sounds reasonable to the team.

title: PIE vs PECL minimal PHP version inconsistency
author: remicollet
body:
In package.xml for PECL minimal version is 7.0 => https://pecl.php.net/package/gRPC

In composer.json for PIE minimal version is 8.0 => https://packagist.org/packages/grpc/grpc-php-ext


Please clarify

title: MSC build failed
author: torsten48
body:
gRPC commit 8bdf11e5097abecbe6cffb70866625a0b8044948 (tag: v1.81.0)

Windows Server 2025
VS 2026, MSC 19.51.36247.0 (building for x86, 32bit)
cmake/ninja

Abseil commit 5650e9cf76d3be4318d5fa3af38ee483ddfd5e4a (HEAD, tag: 20260526.rc2, tag: 20260526.0, origin/lts_2026_05_26)
Protobuf commit 35cd01f9fe9afbeea38cc7b979a3b6bfcde82c03 (HEAD, tag: v5.35.1-objectivec, tag: v35.1)

```
C:\dev\gi2\extern\grpc\src/core/call/call_filters.h(1635): error C3539: a template-argument cannot be a type that contains 'auto'
C:\dev\gi2\extern\grpc\src/core/call/call_filters.h(1636): error C3539: a template-argument cannot be a type that contains 'auto'
C:\dev\gi2\extern\grpc\src/core/call/call_filters.h(1637): error C3539: a template-argument cannot be a type that contains 'auto'
C:\dev\gi2\extern\grpc\src/core/call/call_filters.h(1638): error C3539: a template-argument cannot be a type that contains 'auto'

MSC/Windows is officially supported !?

I guess it's time to switch to Clang.

title: proto file not found issue when compiled using grpc_tools.protoc.main
author: sri-harish-k
body:
> - You haven't done the fix in protoc.main() 
> - It is just passing the arguments passed to the compiler
> 
> Below is the code snippet from grpc_tools/protoc.py module
> ```def main(command_arguments):
>     """Run the protocol buffer compiler with the given command-line arguments.
> 
>     Args:
>       command_arguments: a list of strings representing command line arguments to
>           `protoc`.
>     """
>     command_arguments = [argument.encode() for argument in command_arguments]
>     return _protoc_compiler.run_main(command_arguments)``` 

 _Originally posted by @sri-harish-k in [#24457](https://github.com/grpc/grpc/issues/24457#issuecomment-4721588322)_

title: [Python] Go client hangs sending unary RPC on long-lived Unix socket connection after ~100k requests (Go v1.57.1, Python grpcio 1.60.0)
author: evanxx2902-cloud
body:
### What is the issue?

We are observing a reproducible hang when a Go gRPC client sends a unary RPC over a long-lived Unix domain socket connection to a Python gRPC server. The issue manifests consistently in **one specific customer environment** after the connection has been open for about 10 days and handled roughly 100,000 requests: a new unary call blocks and never returns. Creating a brand new connection to the same server works immediately.

The problem **does not occur** in our own lab environments, even under higher load (1,000,000+ requests per connection). Because the affected environment belongs to a customer, we have **very limited access and cannot perform extended live debugging** (e.g., no long-running packet captures or pprof sessions). Our diagnosis is therefore based on occasional thread dumps and logs.

### Environment

- **Client:** Go, `google.golang.org/grpc` **v1.57.1**
- **Server:** Python, `grpcio` **1.60.0** (CPython)
- **Transport:** Unix domain socket (local, no network middleboxes)
- **RPCs:** Unary calls
- **Load pattern:** Steady low-frequency traffic, roughly one request every 8–9 seconds over the affected connection

### Symptoms

1. After ~10 days of normal operation, a unary RPC call blocks (the calling goroutine hangs; no timeout is configured).
2. The server’s Python thread stacks (dumped at the time of the hang) show **no deadlock** – all threads appear to be in normal I/O waits or idle.
3. **A brand new Unix socket connection** to the same server process succeeds immediately; only the old connection is affected.
4. The issue reproduces **reliably every ~10 days** in this environment, but never in others.
5. We are unable to collect long-term live data (goroutine stacks, socket buffer snapshots, HTTP/2 frame logs) from the customer system, making deep diagnosis challenging.

### What we’ve checked / tried

- Server-side Python thread dump does not show any handler hang or lock contention.
- No network middleboxes are involved – it’s a local Unix socket.
- No RST or GOAWAY frames are logged by either side at the time of the hang.

### Our current suspicion

We suspect something related to HTTP/2 flow control on the extremely long-lived connection, possibly a lost `WINDOW_UPDATE` frame or a stream leak that exhausts the send window over time, causing the client to block when trying to write a new request. The fact that a new connection works perfectly suggests the problem is isolated to the old connection’s state.

### Questions

1. Are there any known flow-control or connection management bugs in `grpcio==1.60.0` or `google.golang.org/grpc` v1.57.1 that could cause a send-side hang after ~10 days of low-frequency traffic?
2. Given our limited access to the customer environment, is there a recommended **lightweight diagnostic approach** we can implement beforehand (e.g., specific debug logging, metrics, or connection lifecycle monitoring) that would capture the necessary information when the hang occurs next time?
3. Would upgrading the server to the latest `grpcio` (e.g., 1.65.x) and/or the client to the latest `google.golang.org/grpc` be the most sensible immediate step, even without a definitive root cause?
4. If upgrading is the suggested path, are there specific release notes or known fixes that we should reference to justify the update to the customer?

Any pointers, known issues, or diagnostic recommendations for such constrained environments would be greatly appreciated. Thank you.

title: [C++] Expose and install health.proto in standard CMake installation targets
author: b-corry
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.
When building C++ clients that consume the standard gRPC Health Checking Protocol, developers need access to the client-side stubs (`Health::NewStub`) and protobuf message classes (`HealthCheckRequest`, `HealthCheckResponse`).

Currently, the standard gRPC C++ CMake installation layout does not export or install:
1. The raw `health.proto` file (e.g., to a `share/grpc/` directory).
2. The generated C++ stub headers (`health.pb.h` and `health.grpc.pb.h`).
3. A client-side library containing the compiled symbols for the health check service.

As a result, downstream projects and package managers (e.g. `conda-forge`) that package `libgrpc`/`grpc-cpp` from the standard install targets do not contain any files relating to `health.proto`, making it impossible to write C++ client channel health checks without checking in/copying the `.proto` file manually.

### Describe the solution you'd like
We would like upstream gRPC to install `health.proto` (and other standard service `.proto` files) to a standard path (e.g. `${CMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX}/share/grpc/protos/`) as part of the standard CMake installation targets. 

This would allow package managers to bundle the proto files, making them easily queryable or importable by downstream CMake build configurations using standard CMake directories.

### Describe alternatives you've considered
1. **Copy-pasting the `health.proto` file:** This is our current workaround, but it forces downstream projects to check in third-party files and maintain them manually.
2. **Exporting pre-compiled C++ client stubs:** Compiling and exporting a public client-side health target (e.g., `gRPC::grpcpp_health_client` containing the compiled stubs and headers) as part of the public C++ API.

### Additional context
This problem is further demonstrated by the official C++ health check example:
- [grpc/examples/cpp/health/CMakeLists.txt](https://github.com/grpc/grpc/blob/0a8376ecfc0aa1e56a8b77ab29852c3c14b81243/examples/cpp/health/CMakeLists.txt#L26-L60)

In this example, the build assumes access to the full gRPC source code repository (`gRPC_SOURCE_DIR`) to find and compile `health.proto` locally. However, this is not possible when consuming gRPC via a precompiled package manager.

title: [CI] Some RBE windows tests won't build with bazel 8.7.0 + rules_go
author: yuanweiz
body:
I noticed this when upgrading bazel from 8.0.1 -> 8.7.0. And example is https://btx.cloud.google.com/invocations/e80bf291-6374-4ee2-9518-6680e8017988/details

some intermediate target `@@rules_go++go_sdk+envoy_api__download_0//:builder` can't be build remotely and that causes other windows tests to fail. (but local build works fine).

```
ERROR: T:/_bazel_output/pjdymepj/external/rules_go++go_sdk+envoy_api__download_0/BUILD.bazel:79:15: GoToolchainBinaryBuild external/rules_go++go_sdk+envoy_api__download_0/builder.exe [for tool] failed: (Exit 34): mandatory output external/rules_go++go_sdk+envoy_api__download_0/gotmp was not created
java.io.IOException: mandatory output external/rules_go++go_sdk+envoy_api__download_0/gotmp was not created

	at com.google.devtools.build.lib.remote.RemoteExecutionService.downloadOutputs(RemoteExecutionService.java:1522)

	at com.google.devtools.build.lib.remote.RemoteSpawnRunner.downloadAndFinalizeSpawnResult(RemoteSpawnRunner.java:494)

	at com.google.devtools.build.lib.remote.RemoteSpawnRunner.lambda$exec$2(RemoteSpawnRunner.java:334)

	at com.google.devtools.build.lib.remote.Retrier.execute(Retrier.java:245)

	at com.google.devtools.build.lib.remote.RemoteRetrier.execute(RemoteRetrier.java:120)

	at com.google.devtools.build.lib.remote.RemoteRetrier.execute(RemoteRetrier.java:109)

	at com.google.devtools.build.lib.remote.RemoteSpawnRunner.exec(RemoteSpawnRunner.java:275)

	at com.google.devtools.build.lib.exec.AbstractSpawnStrategy.exec(AbstractSpawnStrategy.java:161)

	at com.google.devtools.build.lib.exec.AbstractSpawnStrategy.exec(AbstractSpawnStrategy.java:121)

	at com.google.devtools.build.lib.exec.SpawnStrategyResolver.exec(SpawnStrategyResolver.java:46)

	at com.google.devtools.build.lib.analysis.actions.SpawnAction.execute(SpawnAction.java:253)

	at com.google.devtools.build.lib.skyframe.SkyframeActionExecutor$ActionRunner.executeAction(SkyframeActionExecutor.java:1186)

	at com.google.devtools.build.lib.skyframe.SkyframeActionExecutor$ActionRunner.run(SkyframeActionExecutor.java:1089)

	at com.google.devtools.build.lib.skyframe.ActionExecutionState.runStateMachine(ActionExecutionState.java:166)

	at com.google.devtools.build.lib.skyframe.ActionExecutionState.getResultOrDependOnFuture(ActionExecutionState.java:95)

	at com.google.devtools.build.lib.skyframe.SkyframeActionExecutor.executeAction(SkyframeActionExecutor.java:566)

	at com.google.devtools.build.lib.skyframe.ActionExecutionFunction.checkCacheAndExecuteIfNeeded(ActionExecutionFunction.java:946)

	at com.google.devtools.build.lib.skyframe.ActionExecutionFunction.computeInternal(ActionExecutionFunction.java:377)

	at com.google.devtools.build.lib.skyframe.ActionExecutionFunction.compute(ActionExecutionFunction.java:218)

	at com.google.devtools.build.skyframe.AbstractParallelEvaluator$Evaluate.run(AbstractParallelEvaluator.java:467)

	at com.google.devtools.build.lib.concurrent.AbstractQueueVisitor$WrappedRunnable.run(AbstractQueueVisitor.java:435)

	at java.base/java.util.concurrent.ForkJoinTask$RunnableExecuteAction.compute(Unknown Source)

	at java.base/java.util.concurrent.ForkJoinTask$RunnableExecuteAction.compute(Unknown Source)

	at java.base/java.util.concurrent.ForkJoinTask$InterruptibleTask.exec(Unknown Source)

	at java.base/java.util.concurrent.ForkJoinTask.doExec(Unknown Source)

	at java.base/java.util.concurrent.ForkJoinPool$WorkQueue.topLevelExec(Unknown Source)

	at java.base/java.util.concurrent.ForkJoinPool.runWorker(Unknown Source)

	at java.base/java.util.concurrent.ForkJoinWorkerThread.run(Unknown Source)
```

`gotmp` is a folder created on windows hosts by `rules_go` to dynamically generate repositories, see [go/private/rules/binary.bzl](https://github.com/bazel-contrib/rules_go/blob/master/go/private/rules/binary.bzl) . Not sure why it suddenly becomes broken on 8.7.0 only (i've tested from 8.0 through 8.6 and non of them has this issue). I suspect 8.7 introduced some [changes](https://github.com/bazelbuild/bazel/releases/tag/8.7.0) to RBE but I don't have time to dive deeper.

title: Ruby C extension crashes with `completion_queue.cc:344` check failure on interrupted pluck
author: ganesh-tn
body:
### What version of gRPC and what language are you using?
Version: grpc-1.81.0
Language: Ruby
### What operating system (Linux, Windows,...) and version?
OS: Linux (Ubuntu 20.04 compatible environment)
### What runtime / compiler are you using (e.g. python version or version of gcc)
Runtime: Ruby 3.3.8
### What did you do?
We ran gRPC Ruby QPS workers in a load test scenario where calls are cancelled or interrupted during execution or shutdown. Specifically, running the `ruby_protobuf_sync_streaming_ping_pong` scenario using `qps_json_driver`.

### What did you expect to see?
The Ruby worker should shut down cleanly without crashing after the test completes or is cancelled.

### What did you see instead?
The Ruby worker process crashes with a segmentation fault or a fatal check failure in gRPC core:

> `F0000 00:00:1780038769.193187  979624 completion_queue.cc:344] Check failed: completed_head.next == reinterpret_cast<uintptr_t>(&completed_head)`

This happens because Ruby exceptions (like `GRPC::Cancelled`) interrupt the thread while it is waiting in `rb_thread_call_without_gvl` to pluck an event in `rb_completion_queue_pluck`. The thread jumps out of the C loop directly, leaving the event in the completion queue undrained, which triggers the assertion when the queue is destroyed.

### Anything else we should know about your project / environment?
The issue was exposed after updating the Ruby version to 3.3 in the test environment Dockerfiles.

title: Ruby QPS worker fails with `NoMethodError` on Facter and deadlock on streaming shutdown
author: ganesh-tn
body:
### What version of gRPC and what language are you using?
Version: grpc-1.81.0
Language: Ruby
### What operating system (Linux, Windows,...) and version?
OS: Linux
### What runtime / compiler are you using (e.g. python version or version of gcc)
Runtime: Ruby 3.3.8
### What did you do?
Ran the Ruby QPS worker (`qps/worker.rb`) in a test environment where `Facter` does not provide a processors fact or it returns `nil`. Also ran a streaming ping-pong scenario that requires clean shutdown.

### What did you expect to see?
The worker should successfully determine the CPU count or fallback gracefully.
The streaming test should complete and threads should join cleanly on shutdown.
### What did you see instead?

1. A `NoMethodError` occurred in `worker.rb`:

> ruby
> qps/worker.rb:36:in `cpu_cores': undefined method `[]' for nil (NoMethodError)
>     Facter.value('processors')['count']

2. A deadlock occurred in `client.rb` during `streaming_ping_ponger` shutdown. The thread was blocked waiting on responses that would never arrive because the sentinel was not pushed to the queue from the `shutdown` method outside the loop.

### Anything else we should know about your project / environment?
These issues were also observed after updating to Ruby 3.3.

title: Publish pre-built grpcio wheels for ppc64le and s390x on PyPI
author: joao00001
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

Pre-built grpcio wheels are not available on PyPI for ppc64le and s390x architectures. Building from source requires GRPC_PYTHON_BUILD_SYSTEM_OPENSSL=1 (since BoringSSL does not support these architectures) and takes over 1 hour under QEMU emulation, making it impractical for CI/CD packaging pipelines.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Publish pre-built grpcio wheels to PyPI for ppc64le and s390x architectures, similar to what was done for aarch64 (arm64).

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Building from source on every packaging run, impractical due to build times (1h+ under emulation, 30-45min on native hardware).
Out-of-tree packaging (self-hosting wheels in a private repository, similar to the piwheels model), viable but adds maintenance overhead for downstream packagers.
### Additional context

This was previously discussed in #30492 (2022) and #23797. The gRPC team suggested out-of-tree packaging as a workaround at the time. We are reopening this to check if there has been any progress or if there is appetite to add official support, given that aarch64 wheels were successfully added since then as a precedent.

title: Feature request: Select valid cert when tie breaking
author: AntonKanug
body:
<!--
PLEASE DO NOT POST A QUESTION HERE.
This form is for bug reports and feature requests ONLY!

For general questions and troubleshooting, please ask/look for answers at StackOverflow, with "grpc" tag: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/tagged/grpc

For questions that specifically need to be answered by gRPC team members, please ask/look for answers at grpc.io mailing list: https://groups.google.com/forum/#!forum/grpc-io

Issues specific to *grpc-java*, *grpc-go*, *grpc-node*, *grpc-dart*, *grpc-web* should be created in the repository they belong to (e.g. https://github.com/grpc/grpc-LANGUAGE/issues/new)
-->

### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.
See https://github.com/envoyproxy/envoy/issues/45210 for context.

 When both expired and valid root certs exist in the chain, invalid certificate can get picked failing the handshake.

### Describe the solution you'd like
Select valid cert when tie breaking (which might require a boringssl change). Alternatively, allow configuring [X509_V_FLAG_NO_CHECK_TIME](https://docs.openssl.org/1.1.1/man3/X509_VERIFY_PARAM_set_flags/#verification-flags:~:text=X509_V_FLAG_NO_CHECK_TIME) via [TlsCredentialsOptions](https://github.com/grpc/grpc/blob/master/include/grpcpp/security/tls_credentials_options.h#L39) with a new option like `check_validity_period: false` (default true)
